quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Double-slit interference"?

options.text: Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies
Accelerate charged particles to high energies using electric fields and steer with magnetic fields; colliders probe fundamental particles
Bright fringes: d sin θ = mλ; dark fringes: d sin θ = (m+½)λ; d = slit separation; central bright fringe is widest

quiz_questions.explanation: "Double-slit interference" is defined as: Bright fringes: d sin θ = mλ; dark fringes: d sin θ = (m+½)λ; d = slit separation; central bright fringe is widest. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Single-slit diffraction"?

options.text: Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
P + ½ρv² + ρgy = constant; higher velocity means lower pressure; the basis for lift, venturi effects, and flow measurement
v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Single-slit diffraction" is defined as: Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Thin-film interference"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
Path difference = 2nt (n = film index, t = thickness); phase shifts at boundaries with higher-n materials; produces colored patterns
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each
Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: "Thin-film interference" is defined as: Path difference = 2nt (n = film index, t = thickness); phase shifts at boundaries with higher-n materials; produces colored patterns. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Diffraction grating"?

options.text: F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)
V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Diffraction grating" is defined as: Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rayleigh criterion"?

options.text: d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
θ_min = 1.22λ/D; minimum angle of resolution for a circular aperture of diameter D; larger aperture = better resolution
1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rayleigh criterion" is defined as: θ_min = 1.22λ/D; minimum angle of resolution for a circular aperture of diameter D; larger aperture = better resolution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Bright fringes: d sin θ = mλ; dark fringes: d sin θ = (m+½)λ; d = slit separation; central bright fringe is widest"?

options.text: Double-slit interference
Ohm's law
Photon emission
Curie temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Double-slit interference".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern"?

options.text: E-V relationship
Wave interference
Single-slit diffraction
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Single-slit diffraction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Path difference = 2nt (n = film index, t = thickness); phase shifts at boundaries with higher-n materials; produces colored patterns"?

options.text: Resonance
Pair production
Energy levels
Thin-film interference

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Thin-film interference".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)"?

options.text: Quantum superposition
Emission spectrum
Law of reflection
Diffraction grating

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Diffraction grating".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "θ_min = 1.22λ/D; minimum angle of resolution for a circular aperture of diameter D; larger aperture = better resolution"?

options.text: Kirchhoff's loop rule
Beats
Rayleigh criterion
Bernoulli's equation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rayleigh criterion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Interference and Diffraction is accurate?

options.text: Rutherford's gold foil experiment proved atoms have tiny, dense, positive nuclei.
Interference and Diffraction quantifies wave pattern formation.
The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Interference and Diffraction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Like mirrors, the thin lens equation 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f applies (with sign conventions).
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature: constructive interference occurs at d sin θ = mλ (bright fringes, m = 0,±1,±2,...); destructive at d sin θ = (m+½)λ (dark fringes).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Interference and Diffraction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).
Single-slit diffraction: minima at a sin θ = mλ (m = ±1,±2,...).
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.
When E and B are perpendicular, a velocity selector filters particles: only those with v = E/B pass through undeflected (electric and magnetic forces balance).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Interference and Diffraction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Thin-film interference involves path length differences in reflected light: constructive or destructive depending on the film thickness, wavelength, and indices of refraction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Interference and Diffraction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Interference and Diffraction as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference: a microphone detects ambient sound, and speakers produce the inverted wave to cancel noise
CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Velocity selectors in mass spectrometers: only ions with the correct speed enter the separation chamber, improving measurement accuracy
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Interference and Diffraction applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Radio telescope arrays (like the VLA) combine signals from many dishes using interference principles to achieve resolution equivalent to a single dish spanning kilometers
Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law
Charged particle therapy: proton beams for cancer treatment are steered using electromagnetic forces to precisely target tumors deep in the body

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Interference and Diffraction?

options.text: Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Electron microscopes achieve resolutions of ~0.1 nm by using electron 'lenses' (magnetic fields) instead of optical lenses—visualizing individual atoms
Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Interference and Diffraction in practice?

options.text: Refrigerators work as reverse heat engines, moving heat from cold interior to warm exterior—they require work input, consistent with the second law
LED circuits: resistors are placed in series with LEDs to limit current (V_supply - V_LED)/R = I, preventing the LED from burning out
CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Interference and Diffraction.
